Like, so I think there's a couple of different things, right?
Like, one thing is just, like, identifying competitive gaps.
So that's one thing that's, like, fairly straightforward because you're trying to understand, like, what they're showing up for and, you know, what are their opportunities.
And so a lot of what we're doing, there's, like, two ways to think about competitive gaps.
One is, like, once we map.

The audience, and you come up with sample queries, then you start to monitor those queries, and then you can go into the list of all the sources, aka every URL that's cited for those queries, and all the queries you're monitoring, and then what we do is we take those URLs, and we do kind of almost like a working backwards to see, do we have anything currently that kind of matches that semantically or similar to that, and if not, then that's an opportunity, essentially an assignment that we'll flag in our backlog, right, that we can prioritize and go tackle.
So that's one way, that's the like more recent way.

The more traditional way is like, as we're coming up with these queries, and we're identifying mindshare competitors and direct competitors, then we're looking at all their evergreen content, aka not their product pages or feature pages, and then we're looking at every single keyword they rank for, and that those pages rank for, and we see which ones are the ones that your site doesn't rank for at all, and those are net new assignments.
And then we look at the ones that you do have content already, and where you're getting.
Getting less traffic than the potential, right?
And those are refresh opportunities as well, right?
So that's like on the competitive opportunity, you know, research.
And then the actual, actual, like competitive as far as like comparison content, then like we were like really good at that.
